source_paper_title: Inexact Bregman Proximal Gradient Method and Its Inertial Variant with Absolute and Partial Relative Stopping Criteria
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Yang_2026, title={Inexact Bregman Proximal Gradient Method and Its Inertial Variant with Absolute and Partial Relative Stopping Criteria}, volume={51}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2023.0328}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2023.0328}, number={1}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Yang, Lei and Toh, Kim-Chuan}, year={2026}, month=jan, pages={686–714} }

Although this requirement can be satisfied in many cases (see two examples in the following subsection), it may limit the applicability of (ReSC). Note that Bello-Cruz et al. [18] recently incorporated a full relative-type error rule in the FISTA, which can accommodate a nonzero error \(\Delta_k\). Thus, it would be interesting to explore whether the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) can be removed in our methods, which we will leave for future research.
title: Remove zero-gradient error requirement in partial relative inexact Bregman proximal gradient
background_latex:
Let \(E\) be a finite-dimensional Euclidean space, \(Q\subseteq E\) a closed convex set, and consider the convex composite optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\; F(x):=P(x)+f(x),
\]
where \(P:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) is proper closed convex (possibly nonsmooth) and \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is convex and differentiable on \(\operatorname{int}Q\).

Let \(\phi:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) be a proper closed strictly convex Legendre-type kernel with \(\operatorname{dom}\phi=Q\), differentiable on \(\operatorname{int}Q\). The associated Bregman distance is
\[
D_\phi(x,y):=\phi(x)-\phi(y)-\langle \nabla\phi(y),x-y\rangle.
\]
Assume a (restricted) relative smoothness condition: there exists \(L\ge 0\) and a closed convex \(X\supseteq \operatorname{dom}P\cap\operatorname{dom}\phi\) such that for all \(x\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\) and \(y\in X\cap Q\),
\[
f(y)\le f(x)+\langle \nabla f(x),y-x\rangle+L D_\phi(y,x).
\]

For a tolerance \(\delta\ge 0\), the \(\delta\)-subdifferential of \(P\) at \(\bar x\in\operatorname{dom}P\) is
\[
\partial_\delta P(\bar x):=\{d\in E: P(u)\ge P(\bar x)+\langle d,u-\bar x\rangle-\delta\ \forall u\in E\}.
\]

At iterate \(x_k\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\), the (exact) Bregman proximal-gradient subproblem with parameter \(\lambda>0\) is
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\; P(x)+\langle \nabla f(x_k),x-x_k\rangle+\lambda D_\phi(x,x_k).
\]
In the paper's inexact framework, one computes \(x_{k+1}\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\), \(\tilde x_{k+1}\in \operatorname{dom}P\cap Q\), and errors \((\Delta_k,\delta_k)\) satisfying the approximate optimality inclusion
\[
\Delta_k\in \partial_{\delta_k}P(\tilde x_{k+1})+\nabla f(x_k)+\lambda\big(\nabla\phi(x_{k+1})-\nabla\phi(x_k)\big).
\]
A partial relative-type stopping rule (relative error criterion) controls \(\delta_k\) and the two-point deviation \(D_\phi(\tilde x_{k+1},x_{k+1})\) relative to \(D_\phi(\tilde x_{k+1},x_k)\), but (as currently analyzed) imposes \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\).
problem_definition_latex:
Determine whether the partial relative-type stopping rule can be generalized to allow nonzero \(\Delta_k\) while retaining convergence guarantees for the resulting inexact Bregman proximal gradient method.

Concretely, replace the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in the partial relative-type stopping criterion by an admissible condition permitting \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) (for example, bounding \(\|\Delta_k\|\) in a way that is verifiable during subproblem solves), and establish convergence (e.g., convergence of objective values and/or iterates) under the same overall algorithmic framework.
latex:
Let \(E\) be a finite-dimensional Euclidean space, \(Q\subseteq E\) a closed convex set, and consider the convex composite optimization problem
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\; F(x):=P(x)+f(x),
\]
where \(P:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) is proper closed convex (possibly nonsmooth) and \(f:E\to\mathbb{R}\) is convex and differentiable on \(\operatorname{int}Q\).

Let \(\phi:E\to(-\infty,+\infty]\) be a proper closed strictly convex Legendre-type kernel with \(\operatorname{dom}\phi=Q\), differentiable on \(\operatorname{int}Q\). The associated Bregman distance is
\[
D_\phi(x,y):=\phi(x)-\phi(y)-\langle \nabla\phi(y),x-y\rangle.
\]
Assume a (restricted) relative smoothness condition: there exists \(L\ge 0\) and a closed convex \(X\supseteq \operatorname{dom}P\cap\operatorname{dom}\phi\) such that for all \(x\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\) and \(y\in X\cap Q\),
\[
f(y)\le f(x)+\langle \nabla f(x),y-x\rangle+L D_\phi(y,x).
\]

For a tolerance \(\delta\ge 0\), the \(\delta\)-subdifferential of \(P\) at \(\bar x\in\operatorname{dom}P\) is
\[
\partial_\delta P(\bar x):=\{d\in E: P(u)\ge P(\bar x)+\langle d,u-\bar x\rangle-\delta\ \forall u\in E\}.
\]

At iterate \(x_k\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\), the (exact) Bregman proximal-gradient subproblem with parameter \(\lambda>0\) is
\[
\min_{x\in Q}\; P(x)+\langle \nabla f(x_k),x-x_k\rangle+\lambda D_\phi(x,x_k).
\]
In the paper's inexact framework, one computes \(x_{k+1}\in X\cap\operatorname{int}Q\), \(\tilde x_{k+1}\in \operatorname{dom}P\cap Q\), and errors \((\Delta_k,\delta_k)\) satisfying the approximate optimality inclusion
\[
\Delta_k\in \partial_{\delta_k}P(\tilde x_{k+1})+\nabla f(x_k)+\lambda\big(\nabla\phi(x_{k+1})-\nabla\phi(x_k)\big).
\]
A partial relative-type stopping rule (relative error criterion) controls \(\delta_k\) and the two-point deviation \(D_\phi(\tilde x_{k+1},x_{k+1})\) relative to \(D_\phi(\tilde x_{k+1},x_k)\), but (as currently analyzed) imposes \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\).

Determine whether the partial relative-type stopping rule can be generalized to allow nonzero \(\Delta_k\) while retaining convergence guarantees for the resulting inexact Bregman proximal gradient method.

Concretely, replace the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in the partial relative-type stopping criterion by an admissible condition permitting \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) (for example, bounding \(\|\Delta_k\|\) in a way that is verifiable during subproblem solves), and establish convergence (e.g., convergence of objective values and/or iterates) under the same overall algorithmic framework.
location_in_paper: Page 9, Section 3 (discussion following Algorithm 1, paragraph comparing (AbSC) and (ReSC)).
open_problem_quote: Although this requirement can be satisfied in many cases (see two examples in the following subsection), it may limit the applicability of (ReSC). Note that Bello-Cruz et al. [18] recently incorporated a full relative-type error rule in the FISTA, which can accommodate a nonzero error \(\Delta_k\). Thus, it would be interesting to explore whether the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\| = 0\) can be removed in our methods, which we will leave for future research.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally test candidate relative-error rules allowing \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) on synthetic convex instances and check empirically whether descent inequalities or convergence fail, but the space of possible error rules is open-ended and failures may be instance-dependent.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: A coding-agent loop could search over parametrized families of modified relative stopping rules (bounds involving \(\|\Delta_k\|\), \(\delta_k\), and Bregman distances) and evaluate them by running the method on benchmark instances with a computable convergence/efficiency score. However, translating empirical success into a universally valid rule still requires theoretical proof, and fitness signals may be noisy across problem classes.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
In Yang–Toh’s iBPGM, the partial relative stopping criterion (ReSC) is designed to make the key descent estimate (Lemma 3.1) collapse to a clean telescoping inequality by eliminating the troublesome term \(|\langle \Delta_k,\tilde x_{k+1}-x\rangle|\) in (3.7). This is why the current analysis enforces \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) under (ReSC), while allowing \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) only under the absolute criterion (AbSC) with summable tolerances. The open problem asks for a verifiable replacement of \(\Delta_k=0\) in (ReSC) that still yields convergence under the same relative-smooth/Bregman geometry.

Among the forward citations, the closest partial progress is the 2025 iBPDCA paper, which explicitly permits \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) under relative-type stopping rules that bound a computable combination \(\|\Delta_k\|^2+|\langle\Delta_k,x_{k+1}-x_k\rangle|+\delta_k\) by a multiple of a Bregman distance. In the convex composite specialization (no concave part), this essentially provides an inexact Bregman proximal-gradient-type scheme with nonzero residuals and establishes at least subsequential stationarity; however, its strongest whole-sequence convergence requires additional assumptions (e.g., KL and summability of \(\|\Delta_k\|\)), so it does not fully settle Yang–Toh’s convex convergence guarantees under the original (ReSC) framework.

Several related-tool papers (ripALM and SpinAPG) show, in different geometries, how to control nonzero residuals via relative inequalities (often including cross terms) to preserve Fejér-type descent without requiring exact subproblem solves. The inexact FISTA-like framework with adaptive backtracking similarly demonstrates that a relative error inequality can accommodate nonzero residual vectors while retaining convergence rates, albeit in the Euclidean/Lipschitz setting. Collectively, these works suggest that the right generalization of (ReSC) will likely require a strengthened, computable inequality that couples \(\Delta_k\) to the Bregman step (and possibly a cross term \(|\langle\Delta_k,\tilde x_{k+1}-x_k\rangle|\)) so that the analogue of (3.14) can be recovered. As of the provided forward-citation set, no paper fully resolves the open problem in the precise Yang–Toh setting, so the problem appears to remain open.

sources.title: An inexact Bregman proximal difference-of-convex algorithm with two types of relative stopping criteria
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes an inexact Bregman proximal difference-of-convex algorithm (iBPDCA) for problems \(\min_{x\in Q} f(x)+P_1(x)-P_2(x)\) under a restricted \(L\)-smooth adaptable condition. At each iteration it allows a nonzero optimality residual \(\Delta_k\) and \(\delta_k\ge 0\) satisfying \(\Delta_k\in \partial_{\delta_k}P_1(x_{k+1})+
abla f(x_k)-\xi_k+\gamma_k(
abla\phi(x_{k+1})-
abla\phi(x_k))\) with \(\xi_k\in\partial P_2(x_k)\), together with relative stopping criteria (SC1) \(\|\Delta_k\|^2+|\langle\Delta_k,x_{k+1}-x_kangle|+\delta_k\le \sigma\gamma_k D_\phi(x_{k+1},x_k)\) or (SC2) \(\|\Delta_k\|^2+|\langle\Delta_k,x_{k+1}-x_kangle|+\delta_k\le \sigma\gamma_k D_\phi(x_k,x_{k-1})\). Under Assumptions A–C they prove global subsequential convergence: any cluster point of \(\{x_k\}\) is stationary (Theorem 4.1). Under additional assumptions including KL and eventually \(\delta_k\equiv 0\) plus \(\sum_k\|\Delta_k\|<\infty\), they prove whole-sequence convergence to an \(\ell\)-stationary point (Theorems 4.2–4.3).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to remove the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in a partial relative inexact Bregman proximal-gradient framework while retaining convergence. This paper directly allows \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) and analyzes convergence under relative-type stopping rules that bound a computable combination \(\|\Delta_k\|^2+|\langle\Delta_k,x_{k+1}-x_k\rangle|+\delta_k\) by a multiple of a Bregman distance (SC1/SC2). In the convex composite special case \(P_2\equiv 0\) and convex \(f\), their iBPDCA reduces to an inexact Bregman proximal-gradient method with nonzero \(\Delta_k\) under a relative criterion, thus providing a concrete admissible replacement for \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) and proving at least subsequential convergence. However, their strongest whole-sequence convergence requires extra conditions (KL, summable \(\|\Delta_k\|\), and eventually \(\delta_k=0\)), so it does not fully settle the open problem in the exact generality/assumption set posed there.

sources.title: Inexact FISTA-like Methods with Adaptive Backtracking
sources.theorem_or_result: For Euclidean composite minimization \(\min_x F(x)=f(x)+\varphi(x)\) with \(f\) convex \(L_f\)-smooth and \(\varphi\) proper closed convex, the paper proposes an inexact FISTA framework with adaptive backtracking (Framework 1 / Algorithm 1) based on the relative error rule with delay (RERD): find \((x_k,v_k,\varepsilon_k)\) such that \(v_k\in \partial_{\varepsilon_k}\varphi(x_k)+\tfrac{L_k}{\tau}(x_k-y_k)+\nabla f(y_k)\) and \(\|\tau v_k\|^2+2\tau\varepsilon_k L_k\le L_k((1-\tau)L_k-\alpha_k\tau)\|x_k-y_k\|^2+2\tau\delta_{k-1}L_k\). Under bounded stepsizes and standard FISTA growth conditions (Assumption 2) they prove the accelerated objective rate \(F(x_k)-F(x^*)=O(1/k^2)\) (Corollary 1) and stationarity \(s_k\in\partial_{\varepsilon_k}F(x_k)\) with \(\|s_k\|=O(1/k)\), \(\varepsilon_k=O(1/k^2)\) (Corollary 2), plus convergence of accumulation points to minimizers (Corollary 4).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks to remove the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in a partial relative inexact Bregman proximal-gradient method (relative smoothness + Bregman distance) while retaining convergence. This paper does allow a nonzero residual vector \(v_k\) in the inexact proximal-gradient inclusion and controls it via a relative error inequality (RERD), yielding convergence and even \(O(1/k^2)\) rates, and it also introduces an additional tolerance term \(\delta_k\) (“delay”) to accommodate backtracking. However, the setting is strictly Euclidean (quadratic kernel) with global Lipschitz gradient (not relative smoothness/Bregman), and the inexactness model is not the same as the paper’s \(\Delta_k\in \partial_{\delta_k}P(	ilde x_{k+1})+
abla f(x_k)+\lambda(
abla\phi(x_{k+1})-
abla\phi(x_k))\) with two-point Bregman deviations. Thus it does not directly solve the Bregman/relative-smoothness open problem, but it provides a concrete template for permitting nonzero residuals controlled by a verifiable inequality involving \(\|v_k\|\), \(\varepsilon_k\), and step-dependent distances, which could inspire an admissible \(\|\Delta_k\|\) bound in the Bregman framework.

sources.title: ripALM: A Relative-Type Inexact Proximal Augmented Lagrangian Method for Linearly Constrained Convex Optimization
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes ripALM, a relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method for linearly constrained convex problems, with a verifiable relative stopping rule allowing nonzero subproblem residual \(\Delta_{k+1}\neq 0\). Specifically, at iteration \(k\), \(y_{k+1}\) approximately solves \(\min_y\, L_{\sigma_k}(y,x_k)+\tfrac{\tau_k}{2\sigma_k}\|y-y_k\|^2\) such that
\[2\,|\langle w_k-y_{k+1},\,\sigma_k\Delta_{k+1}\rangle|+\|\sigma_k\Delta_{k+1}\|^2\le \rho\big(\|x_{k+1}-x_k\|^2+\tau_k\|y_{k+1}-y_k\|^2\big),\]
where \(\Delta_{k+1}=\nabla_y L_{\sigma_k}(y_{k+1},x_k)+\tau_k\sigma_k^{-1}(y_{k+1}-y_k)\), \(\rho\in[0,1)\), and \(w_{k+1}=w_k-\sigma_k\Delta_{k+1}\). Under existence of a saddle point, \(\{x_k\}\) converges to a primal optimal solution and objective values converge (Theorem 3.1); under an error bound condition, \(\{(y_k,x_k)\}\) converges with asymptotic Q-(super)linear rate (Theorem 3.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to remove a zero-gradient error requirement (\(\|\Delta_k\|=0\)) in a partial relative stopping criterion for inexact Bregman proximal gradient (BPG) under relative smoothness/Bregman geometry. This paper does not analyze BPG, relative smoothness, or Bregman proximal-gradient subproblems. However, it develops and proves convergence for a genuinely relative-type inexact criterion that explicitly permits nonzero residual \(\Delta_{k+1}\neq 0\) (and even includes a cross term with an auxiliary variable \(w_k\)) without summable tolerances or correction steps. The analysis technique—controlling nonzero residuals via a relative inequality tied to successive differences and deriving Fejér-type recursions—could plausibly inspire an admissible nonzero-\(\Delta_k\) condition for the BPG setting, but no direct generalization is provided here.

sources.title: ripALM: A Relative-Type Inexact Proximal Augmented Lagrangian Method with Applications to Quadratically Regularized Optimal Transport
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes ripALM, a relative-type inexact proximal augmented Lagrangian method for linearly constrained convex problems, with a single relative tolerance parameter \(\rho\in[0,1)\). Its subproblem inexactness is measured by a residual \(\Delta_{k+1}:=\nabla_y L_{\sigma_k}(y_{k+1},x_k)+\tau_k\sigma_k^{-1}(y_{k+1}-y_k)\) and an auxiliary variable \(w_k\), and the stopping rule requires
\[
2\,|\langle w_k-y_{k+1},\,\sigma_k\Delta_{k+1}\rangle|+\|\sigma_k\Delta_{k+1}\|^2\le \rho\big(\|x_{k+1}-x_k\|^2+\tau_k\|y_{k+1}-y_k\|^2\big).
\]
Under existence of a saddle point and mild parameter conditions, Theorem 3.1 proves boundedness, \(\Delta_{k+1}\to0\), convergence of primal objective values, and convergence of \(x_k\) to a primal optimal solution; under an error bound condition, Theorem 3.2 proves convergence of \(y_k\) and asymptotic Q-(super)linear convergence of \((y_k,x_k)\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to remove the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in a partial relative stopping criterion for inexact Bregman proximal gradient (BPG) while retaining convergence. This paper does not study BPG or relative smoothness/Bregman proximal-gradient subproblems directly; instead it develops a relative-type inexactness framework for proximal augmented Lagrangian subproblems. However, it is highly relevant as a technique: ripALM’s analysis shows how to allow a nonzero subproblem residual \(\Delta_{k+1}\neq0\) under a verifiable relative criterion (involving computable progress terms and an auxiliary variable \(w_k\)) without summable tolerances. This suggests a potential blueprint for BPG: replace the hard constraint \(\Delta_k=0\) by a relative residual inequality coupling \(\Delta_k\) with Bregman distances/step norms, possibly introducing an auxiliary sequence analogous to \(w_k\) to obtain Fejér-type descent and boundedness. But the paper does not provide such a criterion or convergence proof for the inexact Bregman proximal-gradient method, so it does not solve the stated open problem.

sources.title: Shadow-point Enhanced Inexact Accelerated Proximal Gradient Method with Preserved Convergence Guarantees
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proposes SpinAPG, an inexact accelerated proximal gradient method for Euclidean composite convex minimization \(\min_x P(x)+f(x)\) with \(
abla f\) \(L\)-Lipschitz. Its inexactness criterion uses a (possibly infeasible) iterate \(x_{k+1}\in E\), a feasible shadow point \(	ilde x_{k+1}\in\operatorname{dom}P\), and errors \((\Delta_k,\varepsilon_k)\) satisfying
- \(\Delta_k\in \partial_{\varepsilon_k}P(	ilde x_{k+1})+
abla f(y_k)+L(x_{k+1}-y_k)\),
- \(\|\Delta_k\|\le \eta_k\), \(\varepsilon_k\le 
u_k\), \(\|	ilde x_{k+1}-x_{k+1}\|\le \mu_k\).
Under weighted summability conditions (e.g. \(\sum_k 	heta_k^{-1}\eta_k<\infty\), \(\sum_k 	heta_k^{-1}\mu_k<\infty\), \(\sum_k 	heta_k^{-2}
u_k<\infty\)), it proves \(F(	ilde x_k)-F^* = O(1/k^2)\) (Theorem 3.1(iii)); with \(	heta_k=(\alpha-1)/(k+\alpha-1)\), \(\alpha>3\), it proves \(F(	ilde x_k)-F^* = o(1/k^2)\) and iterate convergence \(x_k,	ilde x_k	o x^*\) (Theorem 3.2).
sources.connection: The open problem asks to remove the requirement \(\|\Delta_k\|=0\) in a partial relative stopping rule for an inexact Bregman proximal-gradient method under relative smoothness. This paper does allow nonzero \(\Delta_k\) and analyzes convergence, but only for the Euclidean (quadratic-kernel) APG/PG setting with Lipschitz gradient (not general Bregman kernels \(\phi\) nor relative smoothness), and it uses absolute/summable error controls \(\|\Delta_k\|\le\eta_k\), \(\varepsilon_k\le\nu_k\), \(\|\tilde x_{k+1}-x_{k+1}\|\le\mu_k\), not a partial relative Bregman criterion. Thus it does not solve the stated Bregman/relative-smoothness open problem, but it provides a closely related two-point (shadow-point) framework showing how to accommodate \(\Delta_k\neq 0\) in accelerated proximal-gradient analyses, which could inspire an admissible \(\Delta_k\)-bound in the Bregman setting.
